summary: DHH explains why 37signals is hiring a junior programmer at $145,849 — a salary high enough to attract experienced developers — but strictly limiting applicants to those with less than three years of experience. The company is hiring for 'peak promise' rather than existing knowledge, arguing that a programmer with five years of experience has already revealed their trajectory. Junior developers represent untapped potential whose growth curve is still unknown and could be steep. He compares it to NFL scouting, where teams pay premiums for rookies who might become all-stars rather than veterans whose ceiling is known.
key_points: 37signals pays top-10% San Francisco salary for juniors because they're hiring for extraordinary potential, not current knowledge
A programmer with five or more years of experience has already revealed their growth trajectory — what you see is roughly what you'll get
Junior hiring is a deliberate bet on upside: fresh developers are 'essentially all potential' with steep possible growth curves
The approach mirrors sports scouting combines — paying a premium for unproven rookies who could become the best
This is explicitly acknowledged as risky: many promising juniors will fail to reach the level the company is betting on
money_quotes: We're hiring for peak promise — and such promise only remains until it's revealed.
A programmer who's been working professionally for five years has likely already revealed their potential. What you're going to get is roughly what you see.
The trajectory by which they improve has already been plotted.
A programmer who's either straight out of school or fresh off their first internship or short-stint job is essentially all potential.
You draw their line on the basis of just a few early dots, but the line can be steep.
That's the chance you take to land someone extraordinary.
key_insight: Hiring juniors at premium salaries is a deliberate bet on unrevealed potential — experienced programmers have already shown their ceiling, but fresh talent's growth trajectory is still unwritten.
